narrative:
As a Black male applicant, your law school application was evaluated using a predictive model that assesses the likelihood of passing the bar exam on the first try, based on profiles from thousands of past students. The model looked at your academic record, test scores, family background, and other details to forecast your future performance. For you specifically, it calculated an 85.9% probability that you would fail the bar exam initially, which is a very high risk—far above what the school considers acceptable for admission. This led directly to the rejection of your application, as the school prioritizes students predicted to succeed quickly and avoid the challenges of retakes.

The three biggest factors pushing the model toward this failure prediction, in order of influence, were your Year 3 school decile ranking, your LSAT score, and your Year 1 school decile ranking—all of which worked against you by signaling lower academic readiness compared to typical applicants. Your Year 3 decile of 1 (well below the average of about 5.5) had the strongest negative pull, suggesting your later undergraduate performance placed you in the bottom tenth of your peers, a red flag for bar exam rigor. Similarly, your LSAT score of 22 (compared to the typical 37) significantly increased the predicted failure risk, as stronger scores are strongly linked to bar passage in the model's view of past students.

Your Year 1 decile of 1 (versus an average around 5.7) added further concern, reinforcing a pattern of early struggles that the model sees as predictive of ongoing challenges. While factors like your middle family income bracket and full-time student status (common among 93% of applicants) were neutral or typical, they couldn't offset these core academic weaknesses. In contrast to average students—who often enter with mid-range deciles and LSATs in the mid-30s—your profile stood out as higher-risk, aligning more with those who historically needed multiple bar attempts.

This isn't a judgment on your potential or resilience, especially given the unique barriers many Black men face in competitive academic pipelines. To move forward, focus on retaking the LSAT to aim for at least the mid-30s, bolstering your undergraduate record through post-bac courses or a master's program, and building a standout personal statement highlighting your growth story. Reapply after demonstrating these improvements, perhaps targeting schools with strong support for diverse students, and consider bar prep resources early. With targeted effort, you can shift your profile into the successful range.
